title: Isaiah 56
subtitle: Third Isaiah Begins: A House of Prayer for All Nations

sections.header: Verses 1–8 — “My House Will Be Called a House of Prayer for All Nations”

heb.paragraph: "My house will be called a house of prayer (təfillâ) for all nations" (v.7). Jesus quotes this when cleansing the temple (Mark 11:17). Third Isaiah (chs.56–66) begins with radical inclusion. The foreigner who binds himself to the LORD should not say "The LORD will surely exclude me" (v.3). The eunuch should not say "I am only a dry tree" (v.3). God gives them "an everlasting name that will endure forever" (v.5). "Their burnt offerings and sacrifices will be accepted on my altar; for my house will be called a house of prayer for all nations" (v.7). Foreigners and eunuchs — those excluded by Deuteronomy 23:1–3 — are explicitly included. The Ethiopian eunuch of Acts 8 is the living fulfilment.

notes.note: "Let no foreigner say, The LORD will exclude me. Let no eunuch say, I am a dry tree. I will give them an everlasting name." The categories Deuteronomy excluded are now included. The law trajectory moves toward ever-wider inclusion.

"My house will be called a house of prayer for all nations." Jesus quotes this when overturning tables (Mark 11:17). The temple purpose: universal prayer. Jesus rage is not against commerce but against exclusion.

"The Sovereign LORD, who gathers the exiles of Israel, declares: I will gather still others to them besides those already gathered." The gathering is not finished. "Still others" — the door remains open.

refs.note: Jesus quotes 'house of prayer for all nations' when cleansing the temple, indicting its failure to fulfill Isaiah's vision.
The Ethiopian eunuch reading Isaiah represents the fulfillment of foreigners and eunuchs being gathered to God's house.
Paul's argument that Gentiles are no longer strangers but fellow citizens draws on Isaiah's inclusive vision.

hist.context: Third Isaiah (chapters 56-66) addresses the post-exilic community, likely in the late sixth century BC. The inclusion of foreigners and eunuchs reverses Deuteronomy 23:1-8's exclusions, signaling that the restoration transcends ethnic boundaries. The rebuilt temple must serve its original purpose as a house of prayer for all peoples, not a nationalistic shrine.

heb.paragraph: "Israel watchmen (ṣōfeh) are blind, they all lack knowledge; they are all mute dogs, they cannot bark; they lie around and dream, they love to sleep" (v.10). After the inclusive vision (vv.1–8), the rebuke: the leaders fail. Blind watchmen, mute guard-dogs, shepherds who look to their own gain (vv.10–11). "Come, let us get wine! Let us drink our fill of beer! Tomorrow will be like today, or even far better" (v.12). The leaders party while the people perish. The contrast with the universal welcome of vv.1–8 is deliberate.

notes.note: "Blind watchmen, mute dogs, shepherds who lack understanding." Three images of failed leadership: eyes that cannot see, mouths that cannot warn, guides who cannot guide. The shepherd critique: they serve themselves.

"Come, let us drink! Tomorrow will be like today, or better!" The leaders intoxicated optimism echoes 22:13 ("let us eat and drink, for tomorrow we die") but with a different spin: not nihilism but delusion.

refs.note: Ezekiel's indictment of Israel's shepherds who feed themselves parallels Isaiah's attack on blind watchmen.
Jeremiah uses the same watchman imagery, warning that those who refuse to listen will stumble.
Jesus's critique of blind guides continues the prophetic tradition of condemning leaders who cannot see.

hist.context: The 'blind watchmen' and 'mute dogs' represent post-exilic leadership failures. Despite the return from Babylon, the community faced corrupt priests, economic exploitation, and spiritual lethargy. The accusation that leaders love to sleep and dream echoes pre-exilic prophetic critiques, warning that restoration alone does not guarantee righteousness.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVThis is what the Lord says: “Maintain justice and do what is right, for my salvation is close at hand and my righteousness will soon be revealed.ESVThus says the LORD: “Keep justice, and do righteousness, for soon my salvation will come, and my righteousness be revealed.NIVLet no foreigner who is bound to the Lord say, “The Lord will surely exclude me from his people.” And let no eunuch complain, “I am only a dry tree.”ESVLet not the foreigner who has joined himself to the LORD say, “The LORD will surely separate me from his people”; and let not the eunuch say, “Behold, I am a dry tree.”

rows.label: Blind Watchmen — 56:9–12
rows.text: "BLIND watchmen, MUTE dogs." Shepherds serve themselves. "Tomorrow will be like today." Delusion.

hebtext.note: "My house will be called a house of prayer (təfillâ) for all nations" (v.7). Jesus quotes this when cleansing the temple (Mark 11:17). Third Isaiah (chs.56–66) begins with radical inclusion. The foreigner who binds himself to the LORD should not say "The LORD will surely exclude me" (v.3). The eunuch should not say "I am only a dry tree" (v.3). God gives them "an everlasting name that will endure forever" (v.5). "Their burnt offerings and sacrifices will be accepted on my altar; for my house will be called a house of prayer for all nations" (v.7). Foreigners and eunuchs — those excluded by Deuteronomy 23:1–3 — are explicitly included. The Ethiopian eunuch of Acts 8 is the living fulfilment.

"Israel watchmen (ṣōfeh) are blind, they all lack knowledge; they are all mute dogs, they cannot bark; they lie around and dream, they love to sleep" (v.10). After the inclusive vision (vv.1–8), the rebuke: the leaders fail. Blind watchmen, mute guard-dogs, shepherds who look to their own gain (vv.10–11). "Come, let us get wine! themes.note: Mission peaks at 10: house of prayer for ALL nations. Foreigners and eunuchs included. Mark 11:17. Acts 8 (Ethiopian eunuch) fulfils. The widest inclusion in the OT.

coaching.tip: 'My house will be called a house of prayer for all nations' (v. 7). Jesus quotes this when cleansing the temple (Mark 11:17), indicting the money-changers for obstructing Gentile access. Isaiah's vision is spatial: the temple's outer courts belong to the nations. Jesus enforced Isaiah's architecture. The temple controversy isn't about commerce — it's about who gets access to God.

The shift from cosmic hope (ch. 55) to corrupt leadership (56:9–12) is jarring. Third Isaiah (56–66) alternates between soaring vision and gritty reality. The pattern is deliberate: the glorious future hasn't arrived because the present community is broken. Isaiah won't let eschatological hope bypass ethical responsibility. The new creation requires new people, and new people aren't here yet.

prayer_prompt: As God declares His house a house of prayer for all nations, consider asking Him to expand your vision of who belongs at His table — and whether your welcome matches His.
